title: Europe’s China Shock In Perspective
gist: Gavekal's Cedric Gemehl argues EU policymakers' fixation on Chinese imports is overblown post-pandemic hysteria, mocking a claim that 29mn EU jobs are at risk from Chinese overproduction (97.6% of EU manufacturing employment), and noting the EU's goods trade deficit with China nearly doubled from €195bn to €378bn in the five years to April 2026. Paywalled teaser.

insights.claim: EU industry commissioner Stéphane Séjourné claimed 29mn EU jobs are at risk from Chinese overproduction, which Gavekal notes equals 97.6% of the 29.7mn people employed in Europe's manufacturing sector at end-2025, framing the claim as hyperbole.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Suggests EU China-hawk rhetoric is exaggerated; tail-risk of aggressive EU trade defense / tariffs on China may be politically driven rather than evidence-based.
insights.quote: European industry commissioner Stéphane Séjourné recently claimed that 29mn jobs in the EU are at risk from Chinese overproduction. For context, this amounts to 97.6% of the 29.7mn people employed in Europe’s manufacturing sector

insights.claim: In the five years to April 2026, the EU's bilateral goods trade deficit with China almost doubled, from €195bn to €378bn.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: The deficit deterioration is the root driver of EU import fixation; a widening gap raises the probability of EU trade-defense measures against Chinese goods.
insights.quote: the EU’s trade deficit with the Asian export powerhouse almost doubled, from €195bn to €378bn.
insights.locator: EU policymakers’ fixation on Chinese imports

insights.claim: Gavekal characterizes EU policymakers' fixation on Chinese imports as a post-pandemic development largely stemming from the rapid deterioration in the EU's bilateral goods trade balance with China.
insights.speaker: Gavekal Research
insights.so_what: Identifies the trade balance as the causal trigger of EU political sentiment toward China; trade-balance trends become a leading indicator of EU policy posture.
insights.quote: EU policymakers’ fixation on Chinese imports is a post-pandemic development, largely stemming from the rapid deterioration in the EU’s bilateral goods trade balance with China.
insights.locator: EU policymakers’ fixation on Chinese imports
